hypothesis: After `residual_vq` and `codebook_topk` both underperformed at `K=24`, a deeper discrete coarse stage is the next fundamentally different architecture worth testing: `two_code_residual_vq` may recover more shared structure before sparse correction and produce a better mid-K FVU/K tradeoff than the single-code discrete families.
summary: Run a pure architecture comparison at `K=24, EF=12` with `two_code_residual_vq` under the pinned recipe `OPTIMIZER=signum`, `LR=3.2e-3`, `AUXK_ALPHA=0.0`. This follows severe-stagnation policy by trying a genuinely new built-in family rather than another repair or scalar tweak, while keeping the comparison anchored on a frontier-relevant smaller-K slot.
self_review: I did not retry the routed repair because the last round was policy-rejected for coupling code edits into a stagnation round. I also avoided adaptive/bucketed support-selection variants because recent stagnation guidance says to stop minor mechanism tweaks; `two_code_residual_vq` is a cleaner architecture jump and directly follows the earlier discrete-family hypothesis.
notes_to_memory: Round 51 continues severe-stagnation recovery with an architecture-only test of `two_code_residual_vq` after both `residual_vq` and `codebook_topk` lost badly at K=24.
Keep the run pinned at `K=24, EF=12, signum, LR=3.2e-3, AUXK_ALPHA=0.0` so architecture is the only primary variable.
`two_code_residual_vq` tests whether two hard coarse code selections plus sparse residual correction work better than the single-code discrete hybrids already tried.
next_hypotheses: If `two_code_residual_vq` is competitive but slightly worse at K=24, test it at K=16 to see whether the deeper discrete hierarchy helps more under tighter sparsity.
If `two_code_residual_vq` clearly loses at K=24, retire this discrete-deeper branch and move to another fundamentally different family rather than any scalar sweep.
If `two_code_residual_vq` wins at K=24, follow with K=16 or K=32 to map whether the gain generalizes across the mid-K band.
